info.name: auth - Redis reconnect verification
info.description:
Covers every endpoint in `@36node/auth` that touches Redis, so you can verify that the new auto-reconnect / command-timeout / readiness probe behaviour actually holds in production.

## Prerequisites

1. `pnpm dev` once. `UserModule.onModuleInit` auto-creates the default admin user (`admin` / `admin@36node.com`) on first boot, so you do not need to seed anything manually.
2. Import the matching environment file (`auth - local (Redis reconnect)`). All credentials default to the values in `src/config/config.ts`; override them if your `.env` is different.

## Auth model

Only `ApiKeyAuthGuard` is registered globally — every non-`@Public()` route needs the `x-api-key` header and nothing else. JWT bearer tokens are **not** required. `/hello` and `/health` are `@Public()` so they skip the API key check; everything else inherits the collection-level `x-api-key` auth.

## Endpoints covered

| Folder | Endpoint | Redis usage |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 0. Health Probes | `GET /hello` | none (Liveness) |
| 0. Health Probes | `GET /health` | **direct PING** (Redis + Mongo readiness) |
| 1. Login lock | `POST /auth/@login` (good) | reads `loginLock:{login}` |
| 1. Login lock | `POST /auth/@login` (bad) | reads + writes `loginLock:{login}` (`hSet` + `expire`) |
| 1. Login lock | `POST /auth/@login` (trigger lock) | same, fires 403 after `MAX_LOGIN_ATTEMPTS` |
| 2. Cache via cache-manager | `GET /users` | none, but populates `testUserId` |
| 2. Cache via cache-manager | `GET /users/{userId}` (first) | cache MISS → writes `/users/{id}` to Redis |
| 2. Cache via cache-manager | `GET /users/{userId}` (second) | cache HIT → reads from Redis |
| 2. Cache via cache-manager | `PATCH /users/{userId}` | `UnsetCacheInterceptor` → DEL from Redis |
| 2. Cache via cache-manager | `GET /users/{userId}` (after invalidate) | cache MISS again, confirms DEL worked |
| 2. Cache via cache-manager | `GET /namespaces` | none, but populates `testNamespaceKey` |
| 2. Cache via cache-manager | `GET /namespaces/{key}` | same cache-write / cache-hit pattern as users |

## How to verify auto-reconnect

1. Start `auth` with `pnpm dev` and a healthy local Redis + Mongo. Default admin user is auto-created.
2. Run the whole collection once (top-to-bottom) — every request should be 2xx, except the lock-trigger step which is expected to flip to 403 once you cross `MAX_LOGIN_ATTEMPTS`. `GET /health` returns `{ status: 'ok' }`.
3. Stop Redis (`docker stop <redis>` or `redis-cli shutdown nosave`).
4. Hit `GET /hello` — still 200 (it never touches Redis).
5. Hit `GET /health` — should flip to **503** within ~1.5s, body `status: 'degraded'`, `checks.redis.ok=false`, `checks.redis.error` mentions `health.ping timed out after 1500ms`.
6. Hit any request in folder 2 (e.g. `GET /users/{userId}`) — should fail fast (not hang!) with a `... timed out after 2000ms` style error in the server log. **This is the proof that command-level timeouts are doing their job.**
7. Restart Redis.
8. Hit `GET /health` again — within a few seconds it flips back to **200** without restarting `auth`. **This is the proof that auto-reconnect works.**
9. Re-run folder 1 and folder 2 — everything should pass again, login-lock state is preserved (if Redis persistence was on) or cleared (if not).

Tip: in Postman Runner, set `GET /health` to iterate 30× with 1000ms delay during the outage window to watch the transition live.

item.name: 0. Health Probes
item.description: Kubernetes-style probes. `/hello` is the Liveness probe (should never touch dependencies); `/health` is the Readiness probe (fans out to Redis + Mongo with a 1.5s timeout per dependency).

request.description: Liveness probe. Does NOT touch Redis or Mongo. Returns 200 as long as the HTTP server is responsive.

Readiness probe. Pings Redis and Mongo concurrently with a 1.5s budget each. Returns 200 + `status='ok'` when both are reachable, 503 + `status='degraded'` otherwise. Use this in K8s readinessProbe so that an unhealthy pod is pulled out of Service Endpoints without being restarted.

During reconnect verification, set Postman Runner to iterate this request 30× with a 1s delay to watch the transition between 200 ↔ 503.

item.name: 1. Redis direct - login lock (loginLock:*)
item.description: `AuthService.isLocked` / `AuthService.lock` use the `REDIS_CLIENT` directly via `hGetAll` / `hSet` / `expire`. Failed logins increment a per-login counter; once it reaches `MAX_LOGIN_ATTEMPTS` (default 5) the next `/auth/@login` returns 403 until the key expires (`LOGIN_LOCK_IN_S`, default 60s).

request.description: Exercises the Redis READ path (`hGetAll loginLock:{login}`) to check that the account is not locked. A successful login also clears the in-flight session state, so this is your 'happy path' baseline.

Exercises the Redis WRITE path. Each call:
1. `hGetAll loginLock:{login}` — read attempts so far
2. `hSet loginLock:{login} attempts=<n+1> lastAttempt=<ts>` — increment
3. `expire loginLock:{login} <LOGIN_LOCK_IN_S>` — refresh TTL

Expect HTTP 401 (`AUTH_FAILED`). Repeat MAX_LOGIN_ATTEMPTS times to trigger the lock — see next request.

item.name: POST /auth/@login (trigger lock) — run 5× then this returns 403

request.description:
Tip: open Postman Runner, select this request, set Iterations = 6. After 5 failures the 6th call returns 403 with `code=TOO_MANY_LOGIN_ATTEMPTS`. Wait `LOGIN_LOCK_IN_S` (default 60s) for the key to expire, or `redis-cli DEL loginLock:admin` to clear manually.

This is the strongest end-to-end proof that the Redis WRITE + EXPIRE + READ round-trips really happen — if `hSet` silently no-ops you'll never reach 403.

WARNING: this also locks out the `admin` account briefly, which will start failing the 'correct password' request above too. Either wait for the TTL or `DEL loginLock:admin` before re-running the collection from the top.

item.name: 2. Redis via cache-manager (SetCache / UnsetCache)
item.description:
`GET /users/:userId` and `GET /namespaces/:key` are decorated with `SetCacheInterceptor` + `CacheTTL(24*3600)`, which stores the JSON response in Redis (via `cache-manager-redis-yet`). The corresponding `PATCH` / `DELETE` endpoints carry `UnsetCacheInterceptor`, which calls `cacheManager.del(...)` to invalidate.

All requests below only need the collection-level `x-api-key` header — no bearer token required.

request.description: Pulls one user and stores its id in `testUserId` for the cache requests below. Reads Mongo, not Redis — useful as a baseline that the API is alive even when Redis is down.

item.name: GET /users/{userId} (first hit → cache MISS, writes Redis)

request.description:
First call after server start (or after the PATCH below) is a cache MISS. The `SetCacheInterceptor` writes the response to Redis under key `/users/{id}` with TTL 24h.

Verify with `redis-cli KEYS '/users/*'` — you should see the new key right after running this request.

item.name: GET /users/{userId} (second hit → cache HIT, reads Redis)

request.description:
Identical request — but the response now comes from Redis instead of Mongo (cache hit). Compare server log: no Mongo `User.findOne` should show up.

When Redis is degraded, this is the exact request that historically would hang forever. With the new command-level timeout proxy it fails fast (~2s) instead, falling back to the Mongo query (cache-manager treats cache errors as cache misses).

Triggers `UnsetCacheInterceptor` → `cacheManager.del('/users/{id}')`. Confirm with `redis-cli GET /users/{id}` — it should be `(nil)` immediately after this call.

The write is also reversible: if you don't want to leave a `labels` mutation on the admin user, change the body to an empty object `{}` (PATCH on Nest accepts it but the interceptor still fires).

Third GET after the PATCH. Should re-populate the Redis key. If the response still carries the pre-PATCH state, cache invalidation is broken (and the new value will only appear after the TTL expires).

exec: pm.test('labels include the marker we just wrote', () => {

request.description: Pulls one namespace, captures its key into `testNamespaceKey`. If your DB has zero namespaces this whole subfolder is a no-op — create one via `POST /namespaces` first or skip these requests.

exec: console.warn('no namespaces in DB; namespace cache requests will 404');

request.description: Same pattern as `GET /users/{id}` — first call writes to Redis, subsequent calls hit cache. Server log should show no Mongo lookup on the second call. Skip if `testNamespaceKey` is empty (no namespaces in DB).

Identical request. Response served from Redis. During the Redis outage portion of the verification this request used to hang; with the command-level timeout proxy it now fails fast (~2s) and falls back to the Mongo query.
